From this point forward, David was followed by one trouble after another. Adultery and murder were David's sins; similar sins among his children were the beginnings of his punishment. He was too indulgent to his children. In this way, David could trace the sins of his children to his own misconduct, which must have made the anguish of the chastisement worse. Let no one ever expect good treatment from those who are capable of attempting their seduction; but it is better to suffer the greatest wrong than to commit the least sin.

Observe the aggravating factors of Absalom's sin: he wanted Ammon slain when he was least fit to leave this world. He involved his servants in the guilt. Those servants are poorly taught who obey wicked masters, against God’s commands. Indulged children always prove difficult for godly parents, whose foolish love leads them to neglect their duty to God.

Jonadab was as guilty of Ammon's death as he was of his sin; such false friends they prove, who counsel us to do wickedly. Instead of loathing Absalom as a murderer, David, after a time, longed to go forth to him. This was David’s infirmity: God saw something in his heart that made a difference, else we would have thought that he honored his sons more than God, just like Eli.
